author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Ambrose Teaches that It is God that Does for Man What Pelagius Attributes to Free Will.
title: Ambrose Teaches that It is God that Does for Man What Pelagius Attributes to Free Will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Chapter 51 [XLVI.]—Ambrose Teaches that It is God that Does for Man What Pelagius Attributes to Free Will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Let him lend an ear also to the same godly bishop, who says, in the sixth book of this same book: “The reason why they would not receive Him is mentioned by the evangelist himself in these words, ‘Because His face was as though He would go to Jerusalem.’

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But His disciples had a strong wish that He should be received into the Samaritan town.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: God, however, calls whomsoever He deigns, and whom He wills He makes religious.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: What wise insight of the man of God, drawn from the very fountain of God’s grace!

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: “God,” says he, “calls whomsoever He deigns, and whom He wills He makes religious.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: See whether this is not the prophet’s own declaration: “I will have mercy on whom I will have mercy, and will show pity on whom I will be pitiful;” and the apostle’s deduction therefrom: “So then,” says he, “it is not of him that willeth, nor of him that runneth, but of God that showeth mercy.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Now, when even his model man of our own times says, that “whomsoever God deigns He calls, and whom He wills He makes religious,” will any one be bold enough to contend that that man is not yet religious “who hastens to the Lord, and desires to be directed by Him, and makes his own will depend upon God’s; who, moreover, cleaves so closely to the Lord, that he becomes (as the apostle says) ‘one spirit’ with Him?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Great, however, as is this entire work of a “religious man,” Pelagius maintains that “it is effected only by the freedom of the will.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But his own blessed Ambrose, whom he so highly commends in word, is against him, saying, “The Lord God calls whomsoever He deigns, and whom He wills He makes religious.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: It is God, then, who makes religious whomsoever He pleases, in order that he may “hasten to the Lord, and desire to be directed by Him, and make his own will depend upon God’s, and cleave so closely to the Lord as to become (as the apostle says) ‘one spirit’ with Him;” and all this none but a religious man does.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Who, then, ever does so much, unless he be made by God to do it?
